questions.questionText: You're carrying the puck on a 2-on-1 rush into the offensive zone. The defender is between you and your teammate. What's your best play right now?

options.label: A
options.text: Shoot — the goalie is at the top of the crease so a quick wrister has space.

options.label: B
options.text: Pass to your teammate on the right — the defender has committed to you and the lane is open.

options.label: C
options.text: Deke around the defender to your left and try to go in alone.

options.label: D
options.text: Chip the puck behind the defender and skate around him.

questions.explanation: This is a classic pass-vs-shoot read on a 2-on-1. The defender has committed to the puck carrier — body weight is leaning left, stick is extended toward your side, and his momentum is going with you. That means the cross-ice passing lane is wide open. Shooting against a goalie who is square to you when a teammate is alone on the back door is the lower-percentage play. The textbook read on a 2-on-1 is: if the defender plays the puck, pass; if the defender plays the pass, shoot. Here, the defender is playing you. Move the puck. Concept: cross-ice read, lane support, pass vs shoot.

questions.questionText: Put these decision steps in the correct order for reading a 2-on-1.

options.label: A
options.text: Sequence to order: (1) Identify what the defender is taking away, (2) Cross the blue line with speed, (3) Choose the option the defender is giving you, (4) Execute the play with confidence

questions.explanation: The mistake most young players make is choosing the play before reading the defender. Order matters: enter the zone, read what's available, make the call, commit fully. Pre-deciding before reading is what causes blown 2-on-1s. CORRECT ORDER: 2 → 1 → 3 → 4 (Cross blue line → Identify → Choose → Execute).

questions.questionText: A real 2-on-1 happens in about 2 seconds. What ONE thing should your eyes find first when you cross the blue line?

options.label: A

questions.explanation: Stick first. Always. Coaches who say 'read the play' really mean 'find the stick.'

questions.questionText: Many young players shoot in this exact situation instead of passing. What's the mental error driving that mistake?

options.label: A
options.text: They want the goal for themselves

options.label: B
options.text: They haven't been taught to read the defender

options.label: C
options.text: They see the goalie and forget to look at the defender

options.label: D
options.text: All of the above

questions.explanation: All three are real and they reinforce each other. Players see the goalie because the goalie is the goal — but the defender is the decision. Selfish shots feel good even when they're wrong. And without coaching that names 'read what he's taking away,' the read never becomes automatic.

questions.questionText: True or False: A shot that goes in the net was the right play, even if a pass would have created a better chance.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Outcomes lie. A bad decision can produce a good result, and a great decision can produce a bad result. In hockey, the read is judged by what was available, not what happened. A goal off a bad shot is still a bad shot. A missed pass that should have been a goal is still the right read.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: The hockey term for letting an outcome change how you judge a decision is 'results-oriented thinking' or ___ bias.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: outcome / outcome bias / results / hindsight

questions.explanation: Outcome bias is judging a decision by how it turned out instead of whether it was the right call given what you knew at the time. Smart players (and smart coaches) judge the read, not the result.

questions.questionText: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — Explain in your own words why passing here is the better play than shooting, even though the shot might score.

options.label: A

The shot might score but it's a low-percentage play because the goalie is square. Even if the pass doesn't lead to a goal, it was the right read because it gave my team the better chance.'

questions.explanation: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — this is for your own thinking. Strong answers will mention: (1) the defender's stick took away the shot, (2) the cross-ice pass creates a better scoring chance, (3) the goalie is positioned for you, not for a pass, and ideally (4) the read is judged by what was available, not by the outcome.

questions.questionText: True or False: With the defender taking away the pass, the goalie has to play you straight up — making the shot a higher-percentage play.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Yes — because the goalie now has to play you alone. With the defender's stick in the passing lane, the goalie can't cheat toward your teammate. He has to be square to you, which is what gives you a clean shot.

questions.questionText: True or False: When the defender takes away the pass on a 2-on-1, the goalie's positioning actually helps the shooter.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: When the defender takes away the pass, the goalie can't cheat across. He has to set up for YOU. That gives you a much cleaner look at the net than if the goalie were anticipating a cross-ice pass.

questions.questionText: If this same defender had his stick on the OTHER side (toward you, the puck carrier), what would your best play become?

options.label: A
options.text: Pass cross-ice — your teammate would be open

options.label: B
options.text: Still shoot

options.label: C
options.text: Skate around the defender

options.label: D
options.text: Drop the puck back

questions.explanation: This is the rule: read the defender, do the opposite. Stick on the pass lane = shoot. Stick on you = pass. The same image with the stick flipped becomes a completely different decision.

questions.questionText: MULTI-SELECT: Which of these are true at the same time in this image? (Select all that apply.)

options.label: A
options.text: The defender's stick is in the pass lane

options.label: B

options.label: C
options.text: The goalie is square to the puck carrier

options.label: D
options.text: The defender is leaning forward charging the puck carrier

questions.explanation: All three are true. Defender's stick takes the pass (A). Your shooting lane is clear (B). The goalie is square to you (C). The defender is NOT charging — he's playing position (D is false). Together these three create the conditions that make shooting the right read. NOTE: This is a multi-select — correct answers are A, B, C.

questions.questionText: A real 2-on-1 happens in about 2 seconds. What ONE thing should your eyes find first when you cross the blue line?

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: the defender's stick / where his stick is / the stick / stick position

questions.explanation: Stick first. Always. Once you find the defender's stick, the rest of the read becomes obvious — his stick tells you what he's taking away, and you do the opposite.

questions.questionText: If this same defender had his stick on the OTHER side (toward you, the puck carrier), what would your best play become?

options.label: A
options.text: Pass cross-ice — your teammate would be open

options.label: B
options.text: Still shoot

options.label: C
options.text: Skate around the defender

options.label: D
options.text: Drop the puck back

questions.explanation: Stick on the pass = shoot. Stick on you = pass. Reading what's taken away and doing the opposite is the whole 2-on-1 game. The same image with a different stick position becomes a different decision.

questions.questionText: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — In your own words, why is shooting the right play here?

options.label: A
options.text: Sample answer: 'The defender's stick is in the passing lane, so he's taking away the pass. That leaves the shot open. The goalie has to play me square, so I can pick a corner.'

questions.explanation: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — this is for your own thinking. Strong answers will mention: (1) the defender's stick took away the pass, (2) the lane to the net is open, (3) the goalie has to play you straight up.

questions.questionText: Put these decision steps in the correct order for reading a 2-on-1.

options.label: A
options.text: Sequence to order: (1) Identify what the defender is taking away, (2) Cross the blue line with speed, (3) Choose the option the defender is giving you, (4) Execute the play with confidence

questions.explanation: Same sequence regardless of which way the defender plays. Order: enter the zone, identify what's taken away, choose what's given, execute. The mistake is pre-deciding before you read. CORRECT ORDER: 2 → 1 → 3 → 4.

questions.questionText: A real 2-on-1 happens in about 2 seconds. What ONE thing should your eyes find first when you cross the blue line?

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: the defender's stick / where his stick is / stick position / the stick

questions.explanation: Stick first. Always. The defender's stick tells you what he's taking away — and what he's giving you. That's the whole read.

questions.questionText: Many young players pass in this exact situation when shooting is the better read. What's the mental error driving that mistake?

options.label: A
options.text: They've been told 'always pass on a 2-on-1'

options.label: B
options.text: They haven't been taught to read the defender's stick

options.label: C
options.text: They don't trust their shot

options.label: D
options.text: All of the above

questions.explanation: All three. Players default to passing because coaches over-emphasize 'sharing' the puck. They miss the stick cue because they haven't been trained to find it. They fear shooting because they don't trust the read. The fix is reps with explicit feedback on reading the defender's stick.

questions.questionText: True or False: A pass that leads to a goal was the right play, even if the shot would have been a higher-percentage chance.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Outcomes lie. A pass that becomes a goal can still be the wrong read — if the better play was the shot. Same reasoning as variant A's outcome bias question, just inverted: judge the read, not the result.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: The hockey term for letting an outcome change how you judge a decision is 'results-oriented thinking' or ___ bias.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: outcome / outcome bias / results / hindsight

questions.explanation: Outcome bias works in both directions. A goal off a bad read is still a bad read. A miss off the right read is still the right read. Smart players evaluate the decision, not the result.

questions.questionText: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — Explain in your own words why shooting here is the better play than passing, even though your teammate looks like an option.

options.label: A
options.text: Sample answer: 'The defender's stick is in the passing lane, taking away the pass. The shot is what he's giving me. The goalie has to play me square because he can't cheat toward the pass, so my shot has a clean look at the net. Even if I miss, it was the right read.'

questions.explanation: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — this is for your own thinking. Strong answers will mention: (1) the defender's stick took away the pass, (2) the lane to the net is open, (3) the goalie has to play you square, and ideally (4) the read is judged by what was available, not by the outcome.

images.readTrigger: The composition itself is the read. The defender is positioned deep (25–30 feet ahead, near the hash marks), creating a wide expanse of open ice between the viewer and the defender. There is visible open ice on BOTH sides of the defender — the cross-ice passing lane AND the lane down the middle toward the goalie are both somewhat available. The defender hasn't committed because he's too far back to commit. The correct read is to attack the open ice, force him to commit, then react.
images.distractors: Defender appears 'safe' and well-positioned (might suggest both passing and shooting are equally bad); the goalie is square and ready (suggests shot won't beat him); the teammate is open but covered enough to make the pass feel risky.

questions.questionText: Look at the picture. You have the puck and lots of room. What should you do?

questions.explanation: The defender is far away. He hasn't picked a side yet. You have lots of time — skate with the puck and see what he does. Don't rush!

questions.questionText: You're on a 2-on-1 rush. The defender is sitting deep and hasn't picked a side. What's your best play?

options.label: A
options.text: Shoot from where you are

options.label: B
options.text: Pass cross-ice immediately

options.label: C
options.text: Drive wide into the open ice and force him to commit

options.label: D
options.text: Stop and wait at the blue line

questions.explanation: The defender is sitting back — he hasn't committed to either you or the pass. You have time and ice. The right play is to drive into the space and force him to commit before reacting.

questions.questionText: You enter the offensive zone on a 2-on-1. The defender is sitting deep, stick centered, body squared. He hasn't committed to either lane. What's the highest-percentage play?

options.label: A
options.text: Shoot from where you are — he's too far back to challenge

options.label: B
options.text: Pass cross-ice — your teammate has open ice

options.label: C
options.text: Drive wide and force the defender to commit before reacting

options.label: D
options.text: Drop pass to a trailing player

questions.explanation: The defender is sitting deep, neither pressuring you nor committing to either lane. The space is your asset — attack it. By driving forward with speed, you force him to commit, at which point you read what he's taking away and respond. Pre-deciding a shot or pass against an uncommitted defender is guessing.

questions.questionText: True or False: There's lots of empty ice in front of you in this picture.

options.label: A
options.text: True ✅

options.label: B
options.text: False ❌

questions.explanation: Yes! The black player is far away. There's lots of empty ice. You have time — don't rush!

questions.questionText: True or False: You should always shoot or pass right away on a 2-on-1.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: When you have lots of room, you don't need to do anything fast. Skate with the puck and wait for the defender to pick a side.

questions.questionText: True or False: You should immediately pick either pass or shoot on every 2-on-1.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: When the defender hasn't committed, neither pass nor shoot is clearly open. The right play is to use the open ice — drive forward, force him to commit, then react.

questions.questionText: True or False: You should always force a pass-or-shoot decision on every 2-on-1, even when the defender hasn't committed.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Forcing a read on every 2-on-1 leads to bad decisions. When the defender hasn't given you a clear cue, the skilled play is to wait, attack space, and let the defender commit first.

questions.questionText: Tap the empty space where you can skate.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: the wide open ice in the middle of the frame

questions.explanation: That's the open ice! Skate the puck into all that space. You don't have to pass or shoot right away when you have lots of room.

questions.questionText: Tap the open space where you should skate the puck.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: the wide open ice between you and the defender

questions.explanation: That's the open ice. Skate the puck in there. You'll get closer to the net AND make the defender finally pick what he's covering.

questions.questionText: Tap the open ice you should attack.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: the open ice between the puck carrier and the defender

questions.explanation: That's the open ice you should attack. Carry the puck in there with speed. Once you're closer, the defender has to pick something — and that's when your read becomes clear.

questions.questionText: Tap the area you should attack with the puck.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: the wide open ice between the puck carrier and the defender

questions.explanation: That's the open ice you should attack. Driving the puck into that space accomplishes two things: it gets you to a higher-percentage scoring area AND forces the defender to make a choice. Both are good for you.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: When the defender is far back and hasn't picked a side, the right play is to ___.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: skate / carry it in / wait / be patient / drive wide

questions.explanation: Yes! The defender hasn't picked. So you carry the puck closer until he picks. Then you do the opposite of what he does.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: When the defender hasn't committed on a 2-on-1, the right play is to ___.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: drive wide / attack the open ice / carry it in / be patient / wait for him to commit

questions.explanation: When the defender doesn't commit, the puck carrier has to make him commit. The way you do that is by attacking with speed into the open ice. Once he has to choose, you choose the opposite.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: When the defender on a 2-on-1 hasn't committed, the right play is to ___.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: drive wide / attack the open ice / be patient / force him to commit / carry it in

questions.explanation: When the defender hasn't picked, you have to force him to. Driving wide with speed makes him choose between holding the middle or stepping up. Either way, his choice creates your read.

questions.questionText: Where is the defender's stick pointing?

options.label: A
options.text: On your side (the puck)

options.label: B
options.text: On your teammate (the pass)

options.label: C
options.text: Straight back, not picking yet

options.label: D
options.text: Above his head

questions.explanation: He's just standing there waiting. He hasn't picked the puck OR the pass. So you have to make him pick first.

questions.questionText: When the defender hasn't picked a side, what's the most important thing to read?

options.label: A
options.text: His stick

options.label: B
options.text: How much open ice is between you and him

options.label: C
options.text: His helmet color

options.label: D
options.text: His skate angle

questions.explanation: When the defender hasn't committed, you can't read his stick the same way — there's nothing to read yet. So instead, you read the SPACE. Lots of open ice means you have time. Use it.

questions.questionText: When stick position doesn't give a clear read, what's the next-best cue?

options.label: A
options.text: Body lean

options.label: B
options.text: Gap distance and open ice

options.label: C
options.text: Skating speed

options.label: D
options.text: Eye contact

questions.explanation: When stick position isn't a clear read (because he hasn't committed), the next-best read is the GAP — how much space he's giving you. A big gap means you have time. A small gap means you don't. Read the space when you can't read the stick.

questions.questionText: Why is it sometimes the best play to slow down on a 2-on-1?

options.label: A
options.text: Slow players never score

options.label: B
options.text: When you have lots of room, taking your time gives you a better chance

options.label: C
options.text: Slow is always better

options.label: D
options.text: Coaches like slow players

questions.explanation: When you have lots of room, taking your time is smart. Rushing into a shot or pass when you don't have to is what gives the puck back to the other team.

questions.questionText: What hockey concept best describes the read in this image?

options.label: A
options.text: Soft Area / Quiet Zone

options.label: B
options.text: Patience and forcing the commitment

options.label: C
options.text: Cycle

options.label: D
options.text: Cross-ice read

questions.explanation: An indecisive defender isn't 'good positioning' — he's giving you time. The puck carrier's job is to use that time to get to a better spot, not to rush a play that's not there yet.

questions.questionText: What hockey concept best describes the read in this image?

options.label: A
options.text: Soft Area / patience read

options.label: B
options.text: Cross-ice read

options.label: C
options.text: Net drive

options.label: D
options.text: Cycle

questions.explanation: When the defender has dropped back into a 'soft area' (no pressure on you), your job is to USE that space. The read is patience and attacking ice, not a snap pass-or-shoot decision.

questions.questionText: Why is patience the right play here?

options.label: A
options.text: Patience is for slow players

options.label: B
options.text: Forcing a decision when the defender hasn't committed yet leads to bad reads

options.label: C
options.text: You should always shoot first and ask questions later

options.label: D
options.text: Defenders are always indecisive

questions.explanation: Bad reads come from rushing. When the defender hasn't given you a clear cue, picking pass or shoot anyway is guessing. The skilled play is to keep your options open until the defender tells you what he's giving up.

questions.questionText: Why is patience the higher-percentage play here, even though it might feel like wasting time?

options.label: A
options.text: You're not allowed to make decisions early

options.label: B
options.text: Neither pass nor shoot has a clear edge yet, AND you have the space and time to wait for one

options.label: C
options.text: Coaches don't trust young players to decide

options.label: D
options.text: It looks better in highlight videos

questions.explanation: Two compounding factors. First, an indecisive defender hasn't committed — so neither pass nor shoot has a clear advantage yet. Second, you have time and space. Use both. Patience converts an ambiguous read into a clear one once he's forced to commit.

questions.questionText: True or False: Carrying the puck with speed is one way to FORCE an indecisive defender to commit.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: True. Speed forces the defender to react. When you're carrying with pace into open ice, he has to pick something or you're going around him. The threat of speed creates the read.

questions.questionText: True or False: A skilled puck carrier knows when to wait for a read instead of forcing one.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: The lesson here is that good reads aren't about always knowing the answer — they're about knowing when an answer is and isn't available. When the defender hasn't given you the cue, you don't have a read yet. So you create the conditions for one to appear.

questions.questionText: If the defender suddenly stepped UP and started closing the gap, what would change about your read?

options.label: A
options.text: You should still drive wide

options.label: B
options.text: The read changes — now you might need to chip past him or pass quickly

options.label: C
options.text: You should still shoot

options.label: D
options.text: It doesn't matter

questions.explanation: If the defender steps up, the variant changes — now it's variant D (defender stepping up), and the right play is to chip past him or make a quick pass. The takeaway: the read changes as the defender's behavior changes. Stay open to all options until he picks.

questions.questionText: MULTI-SELECT: Which of these are true at the same time in this image? (Select all that apply.)

options.label: A
options.text: The defender's body is squared and centered — he hasn't committed

options.label: B
options.text: There is open ice on both sides of the defender

options.label: C
options.text: Neither the cross-ice pass nor the shot has a clearly committed defender

options.label: D
options.text: The defender is leaning forward and charging the puck carrier

questions.explanation: All three are true. Defender is centered (A). There's open ice on both sides (B). Neither lane is fully covered (C). Defender is NOT charging — he's reactive, not aggressive (D is false). Together these conditions mean: you have to make him commit before reacting. NOTE: Multi-select — correct answers are A, B, C.

questions.questionText: When the defender is sitting back, what should your eyes find FIRST?

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: the open ice / how much space / the space / the defender's distance / how far back he is

questions.explanation: When there's lots of space, your eyes find the SPACE first. The space tells you you have time. Then once the defender starts to commit, your eyes go to his stick to read what he's taking away.

questions.questionText: If, as you drove forward, the defender suddenly committed his stick to your side (the puck side), what would your best play become?

options.label: A
options.text: Still drive wide — nothing has changed

options.label: B
options.text: Cross-ice pass — he's now taking away the shot, so the pass is what's open

options.label: C
options.text: Still skate — you have all the time in the world

options.label: D
options.text: Drop pass back to a trailing teammate

questions.explanation: If the defender finally committed his stick to the puck side (taking away the shot), the cross-ice pass becomes the read — just like variant A. The takeaway: when the defender finally commits, you're back in textbook 2-on-1 territory. Read what's taken away, do the opposite.

questions.questionText: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — In your own words, why is patience the right play here instead of immediately shooting or passing?

options.label: A
options.text: Sample answer: 'The defender is sitting back and hasn't committed to either side. There's lots of open ice. I should drive into that space with speed, and once he has to commit, I'll do the opposite of what he does.'

questions.explanation: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — this is for your own thinking. Strong answers will mention: (1) the defender hasn't picked a side, (2) there's lots of open ice, (3) you should attack the space and force him to commit before deciding pass or shoot.

questions.questionText: Put these decision steps in the correct order for reading a 2-on-1.

options.label: A
options.text: Sequence to order: (1) Identify what the defender is taking away, (2) Cross the blue line with speed, (3) Choose the option the defender is giving you, (4) Execute the play with confidence

questions.explanation: Same sequence whether the defender commits early or late. Order: enter the zone, identify what the defender is doing, choose the right response, execute. The only difference here is that 'identify' takes longer because the defender is uncommitted. CORRECT ORDER: 2 → 1 → 3 → 4.

questions.questionText: When the defender is uncommitted, what's the most important visual to read first?

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: the gap / the open ice / the space / the distance / how far back he is

questions.explanation: When the defender is uncommitted, the GAP is the read. Big gap = time. Small gap = no time. Once he commits, your eyes go back to his stick. But until then, the gap is what tells you what kind of decision you're in.

questions.questionText: Many young players force a quick shot or pass in this exact situation when patience is the better read. What's the mental error driving that mistake?

options.label: A
options.text: They panic when they have the puck and feel they have to do SOMETHING

options.label: B
options.text: They've been taught only two options (pass or shoot)

options.label: C
options.text: They confuse rushing with playing fast

options.label: D
options.text: All of the above

questions.explanation: All three. Players panic with the puck because they think they have to do something — they don't realize 'wait' is also an action. They were taught a binary (pass or shoot) and don't have the framework for 'force him to commit first.' And rushing makes you feel like you're playing fast, but it's actually playing reactive instead of intentional.

questions.questionText: True or False: A rushed shot that goes in proves that the rush was the right play.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Outcomes lie. A rushed shot that scores doesn't mean rushing was right — it means you got lucky. Patience would have produced a higher-percentage scoring chance. The read is judged by what was available, not what happened.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: The hockey term for letting an outcome change how you judge a decision is ___ bias.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: outcome / outcome bias / results / hindsight / results-oriented

questions.explanation: Outcome bias — judging a decision by its result instead of by whether it was the right call given what you knew at the time. Particularly insidious in patience situations because patient plays sometimes don't lead to scoring chances, and impatient plays sometimes do. The read is what's available; the outcome is variance.

questions.questionText: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — Explain in your own words why patience and driving wide is the better play here than immediately shooting or passing.

options.label: A
options.text: Sample answer: 'The defender is sitting deep and hasn't committed. There's no clear read yet. If I rush a pass or shot, I'm guessing. The smarter play is to drive wide with speed — that forces him to either step up (variant D) or commit to a side (variant A or B). Once he picks, I have a clean read. Patience here is what creates the read, not waiting for one.'

questions.explanation: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — this is for your own thinking. Strong answers will mention: (1) the defender hasn't committed, (2) you have open ice and time, (3) attacking with speed forces him to commit, (4) patience converts an ambiguous read into a clear one, and ideally (5) the read is judged by what was available, not by the outcome.

questions.questionText: True or False: You have lots of time to decide what to do here.

questions.questionText: True or False: You should always slow down and skate around an aggressive defender.

options.label: B

questions.questionText: True or False: When a defender steps up aggressively, you should slow down and try to skate around him.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: When the defender steps up, the gap is closing fast and you have very little time. Slowing down means he hits you. The right move is to get the puck off your stick fast — pass or chip.

questions.questionText: True or False: When a defender steps up aggressively, you should slow down and try to deke past him.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Slowing down against a stepping-up defender means you absorb the hit. You have less than a second — the puck has to move before he closes you. Quick decision, quick execution.

questions.questionText: Tap your friend who needs the puck FAST.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: your white-jersey teammate on the right

questions.explanation: Yes! Pass to your friend fast. The black player can't get to him in time. Quick pass!

questions.questionText: Tap the open ice you should try to get the puck into.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: the open ice BEHIND the defender (between him and the goalie)

questions.explanation: That's where the open ice is — BEHIND the defender. He stepped up to attack you, and that left a HUGE space behind him. Get the puck there fast.

questions.questionText: Tap the area where you want the puck to end up.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: the open ice BEHIND the defender between him and the goalie

questions.explanation: That's the open ice the defender just created when he stepped up. Get the puck there — either by passing to your teammate (who has a clear lane) or chipping past the defender and chasing.

questions.questionText: Tap the area where the puck needs to end up.

options.label: A
options.text: Hotspot target: the wide open ice BEHIND the defender, between him and the goalie

questions.explanation: That's the open ice the defender created when he stepped up. Get the puck there — a chip past him into that space, or a quick pass to a teammate who can attack the now-uncovered middle.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: When the defender is rushing at you, you have to ___ the puck fast.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: chip / pass / pass it / move it / quick pass / chip past him

questions.explanation: When a defender steps up fast, the trick is to use his speed against him. Chip the puck past him or pass quickly — and skate around to where the puck is going.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: When the defender steps up aggressively, you have to ___ the puck before contact.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: chip / chip past him / pass quickly / pass before contact / get rid of it / move it fast

questions.explanation: When a defender steps up, he creates a one-second window. You either move the puck before contact or you lose it. Quick decision, quick execution.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: When the defender steps up aggressively on a 2-on-1, the right play is to ___ the puck before contact.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: chip / chip past him / pass quickly / pass before contact / move it fast

questions.explanation: When a defender abandons the standard 2-on-1 position by stepping up, you can't deke or skate around him — you'd be running into his momentum. The fix is moving the puck before contact: chip it past him or pass before he closes.

questions.questionText: What's BEHIND the defender in this picture?

options.label: A
options.text: Lots of empty ice (because he left the middle when he stepped up)

options.label: B
options.text: Another defender

options.label: C
options.text: The boards

options.label: D
options.text: Your teammate

questions.explanation: When the defender steps up, he leaves the middle wide open. That space behind him is where you want the puck to go.

questions.questionText: When the defender steps up aggressively, what's the most important visual cue?

options.label: A
options.text: His helmet color

options.label: B
options.text: His teammates

options.label: C
options.text: How small the gap is and how fast it's closing

options.label: D
options.text: His stick blade angle

questions.explanation: When the defender steps up, the cue is the GAP — how fast it's closing. You also notice he's not skating backward like a textbook 2-on-1 defender. That tells you he's broken protocol and you need to react fast.

questions.questionText: When a defender steps up aggressively, which physical cue tells you he's broken the textbook 2-on-1 protocol?

options.label: A
options.text: Stick position

options.label: B
options.text: His body angle and the closing speed of the gap

options.label: C
options.text: His skate edges

options.label: D
options.text: His helmet color

questions.explanation: When the defender abandons the standard 2-on-1 position, the cues that matter shift. Stick position becomes secondary. The PRIMARY cues are: how close is he, how fast is he closing, and where is the open space he just left. Body angle (forward vs. backward) is the tell that he's stepped up.

questions.questionText: True or False: When a defender attacks you, he leaves open space BEHIND him.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: When a defender comes at you fast, he leaves a hole behind him. Smart players use that hole. Slow players just get hit by the defender.

questions.questionText: What's BEHIND the defender in this image, and why does it matter?

options.label: A
options.text: It's full of his teammates

options.label: B
options.text: It's wide open — he left it when he stepped up

options.label: C
options.text: It's hard to reach

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing important

questions.explanation: The counterintuitive lesson: an aggressive defender CREATES space. By stepping up to attack you, he leaves a huge area behind him completely uncovered. The puck has to get to that space — fast.

questions.questionText: What hockey concept best describes why an aggressive defender can be exploited?

options.label: A
options.text: Aggression creates space — the defender's commitment leaves the middle open

options.label: B
options.text: Aggression shuts down all options

options.label: C
options.text: Aggression always works against forwards

options.label: D
options.text: Aggressive defenders are always good defenders

questions.explanation: An aggressive defender CREATES space behind himself. By stepping up, he's converted his coverage zone into open ice. The puck's job is to find that space — either via a chip past him or a pass to a teammate who can attack it.

questions.questionText: What's the counterintuitive lesson about aggressive defenders?

options.label: A
options.text: Aggressive defenders are always good

options.label: B
options.text: Aggressive defenders never give up space

options.label: C
options.text: Aggressive defenders create space behind them by stepping up

options.label: D
options.text: Aggressive defenders are easy to skate around

questions.explanation: Aggressive defenders create space behind them. The same speed that lets them attack you also means they can't stop and recover. Use their momentum against them — chip past, or pass to a teammate who's now wide open.

questions.questionText: Why is the chip-past-him or quick-pass play the highest-percentage option here?

options.label: A
options.text: Chips and quick passes are easier to execute than skating

options.label: B
options.text: The defender has both vacated the middle AND given up the ability to recover — his momentum works against him

options.label: C
options.text: Coaches like aggressive plays

options.label: D
options.text: It looks better in highlights

questions.explanation: Two compounding factors. First, the defender has abandoned coverage — the middle is wide open. Second, his momentum carries him AWAY from where the play needs to go. So a chip past him or a quick pass converts his aggression into your scoring chance. The faster the defender is closing, the more advantage you gain by moving the puck before contact.

questions.questionText: True or False: An aggressive defender's own speed can be used against him.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: True. The defender's speed coming AT you can be turned into your speed going PAST him — if you move the puck fast enough. Once the puck is past him, his momentum carries him AWAY from where the play is going.

questions.questionText: True or False: A defender's own momentum can be used against him — once the puck is past, he can't reverse to recover.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: True. An aggressive defender is committing in one direction at high speed. He cannot reverse direction quickly. That means once the puck is past him, he's effectively out of the play.

questions.questionText: If, at the last second, the defender backed off and started skating BACKWARD again, what would change about your read?

options.label: A
options.text: Still chip past him

options.label: B
options.text: The read would change — you'd be back in a textbook 2-on-1 (read the stick)

options.label: C
options.text: It wouldn't change anything

options.label: D
options.text: Always shoot

questions.explanation: If the defender backed off into a textbook position, you'd be in variant A, B, or C — the read changes based on what he does next. The takeaway: defender behavior changes the read, and you have to stay flexible until he commits.

questions.questionText: MULTI-SELECT: Which of these are true at the same time in this image? (Select all that apply.)

options.label: A
options.text: The defender has stepped up and abandoned the textbook backward-skating position

options.label: B
options.text: The gap is small and closing fast

options.label: C
options.text: There's huge open ice BEHIND the defender

options.label: D
options.text: The defender is skating backward in a textbook 2-on-1 position

questions.explanation: All three are true. Defender stepped up (A). Gap is small and closing fast (B). The middle behind him is wide open (C). The defender is NOT skating backward in textbook position — he's broken protocol (D is false). NOTE: Multi-select — correct answers are A, B, C.

questions.questionText: When a defender steps up aggressively, what does your eye need to find FIRST?

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: the gap / how close he is / the closing speed / the small gap / how fast he's coming

questions.explanation: When the defender steps up, the gap closes fast. You have less than a second. The cue is the GAP itself — how close he is and how fast it's shrinking. That's what tells you to move the puck NOW, not in a moment.

questions.questionText: If the defender hadn't stepped up and was instead playing a normal backward-skating 2-on-1, what would change about your read?

options.label: A
options.text: Still chip past him

options.label: B
options.text: The read would be different — you'd be in textbook 2-on-1 territory and would read his stick

options.label: C
options.text: Always shoot

options.label: D
options.text: Always pass

questions.explanation: If the defender hadn't stepped up and was instead playing the textbook position, you'd be in variant A, B, or C — and you'd read his stick. The aggression read only applies when his body has committed forward. Defender behavior dictates the read.

questions.questionText: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — In your own words, why is moving the puck quickly the right play here?

options.label: A
options.text: Sample answer: 'The defender stepped up to attack me, but that left huge space behind him. I don't have time to skate around him — I need to move the puck fast. Either I pass to my teammate before contact, or I chip the puck past the defender and skate around. His own speed becomes my advantage if I'm fast enough.'

questions.explanation: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — this is for your own thinking. Strong answers will mention: (1) the defender stepped up and left space behind him, (2) you have to move the puck fast — pass or chip, (3) using his speed against him.

questions.questionText: Put these decision steps in the correct order for reading a 2-on-1.

options.label: A
options.text: Sequence to order: (1) Identify what the defender is taking away, (2) Cross the blue line with speed, (3) Choose the option the defender is giving you, (4) Execute the play with confidence

questions.explanation: Same sequence whether the defender steps up or plays the textbook — the difference is just speed. With an aggressive defender, the IDENTIFY step happens in milliseconds because the visual cues (closing gap, forward body angle) are sudden and clear. CORRECT ORDER: 2 → 1 → 3 → 4.

questions.questionText: When a defender has stepped up aggressively, what does your eye need to find FIRST?

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: the gap / how close he is / closing speed / how fast he's coming

questions.explanation: When the defender steps up, the gap is the read. How close is he? How fast is he closing? Those answers tell you whether you have time for a quick decision or have to react instantly. Once the gap goes below ~5 feet, the only options are pass before contact or chip past.

questions.questionText: Many young players get hit or turn over the puck in this exact situation. What's the mental error driving that mistake?

options.label: A
options.text: They freeze under time pressure

options.label: B
options.text: They try to skate around the defender (which works against passive defenders, not aggressive ones)

options.label: C
options.text: They don't trust 'losing the puck' even when chipping it past is the right play

options.label: D
options.text: All of the above

questions.explanation: All three. Players freeze under time pressure because they haven't built the pattern recognition to know what to do in a sub-second window. They try to skate around because that's what works against passive defenders. And they don't trust the chip because they think 'losing the puck' is bad — but a chip into open space isn't losing it, it's relocating it.

questions.questionText: True or False: If a chip-past-the-defender play results in a turnover, that means the chip was the wrong read.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Outcomes lie. A turnover off a chip-past doesn't mean the chip was wrong — sometimes plays don't work because of execution, ice conditions, or your teammate not reading the situation. The READ (chip past an aggressive defender) is correct. Whether your teammate gets there in time is a separate variable.

questions.questionText: Fill in the blank: The hockey term for letting an outcome change how you judge a decision is ___ bias.

options.label: A
options.text: Accepted answers: outcome / outcome bias / results / hindsight / results-oriented

questions.explanation: Outcome bias works in both directions in time-pressure plays. A successful chip past an aggressive defender doesn't mean the chip was the only right play. A turnover doesn't mean it was the wrong read. The skill is judging the read, not the outcome.

questions.questionText: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — Explain in your own words why moving the puck quickly is the right play here, even though it might result in a turnover.

options.label: A
options.text: Sample answer: 'The defender abandoned the textbook 2-on-1 position by stepping up. He's closing fast and I have less than a second. I can't deke or skate around because his momentum is coming AT me — I'd just run into him. So I have to move the puck before contact: chip it past him into the open space he just left, or pass quickly to my teammate who's now wide open. His aggression created the space — I'm using it against him. Even if it doesn't work out, the read was right.'

questions.explanation: 📝 NOT AUTO-GRADED — this is for your own thinking. Strong answers will mention: (1) the defender stepped up and broke the textbook position, (2) the gap is closing fast, (3) the open ice behind him is the target, (4) you have to move the puck before contact, and ideally (5) his own aggression creates the space you're attacking.

images.readTrigger: Forecheck pressure on the strong-side wall — find the near-side support before the second forechecker arrives.

questions.questionText: You just retrieved the puck on the right boards in your defensive zone. The forecheck is on you. What's the first read?

options.label: B

options.label: C

questions.questionText: Why is the wall pass to the same-side winger usually the safest first option on a breakout?

options.label: A
options.text: It's the shortest pass and the boards block most defender interference

options.label: B
options.text: It's the longest possible pass for the most distance

options.label: C
options.text: It always leads to a goal

options.label: D
options.text: It's the only option allowed

questions.questionText: The near-side wall pass is jammed up — your winger is checked. What's the next-best option?

options.label: A

options.label: B

options.label: C
options.text: Skate it through center yourself

options.label: D

questions.explanation: When near-side is taken, switch the puck across to your D partner and reset.

questions.questionText: Where should the off-side defenseman be during a near-side breakout?

options.label: A

options.label: B

options.label: C

options.label: D
options.text: Behind the goalie

questions.explanation: Your D partner is your insurance — high middle gives them a clear line of vision and a quick pass option.

questions.questionText: What's the most common reason youth-hockey breakouts fall apart?

options.label: A
options.text: Off-puck players don't move to give the carrier a passing option

options.label: B
options.text: The goalie doesn't help

options.label: C
options.text: Too many players skate too fast

options.label: D
options.text: The boards get in the way

questions.questionText: Which forecheck makes a breakout hardest?

options.label: B
options.text: 1-2-2 with one deep forechecker

options.label: C
options.text: A neutral-zone trap

options.label: D
options.text: No forecheck at all

questions.questionText: What does a stretch pass to your high forward give you?

options.label: A
options.text: A direct outlet that beats every forechecker in one pass

options.label: B
options.text: Almost always a turnover — too risky to ever try

options.label: C
options.text: A guaranteed icing call

options.label: D
options.text: The same thing as a wall pass

questions.explanation: Stretch passes are high-risk, high-reward — call them when you see all three forecheckers below the puck.

questions.questionText: Tap your best first pass option from the right boards.

options.label: A
options.text: [Spot 1, near-side winger] CORRECT — short, low-risk wall pass

options.label: B

options.label: C

questions.explanation: Up the boards to your near winger is breakout 101.

questions.questionText: Tap the forechecker that's the biggest immediate threat.

options.label: A
options.text: [Spot 1, closer forechecker] CORRECT — closing the wall pass first

options.label: B
options.text: [Spot 2, high forechecker] WRONG — secondary threat (takes away stretch)

options.label: C

questions.explanation: Closer forechecker = first decision. Read them before the puck even arrives on your stick.

questions.questionText: Tap where the off-side defenseman should be positioned.

options.label: A

options.label: B
options.text: [Spot 2, low / behind goalie] WRONG — on top of goalie

options.label: C

questions.questionText: Tap your safety-valve target if every option breaks down.

options.label: A

options.label: B
options.text: [Spot 2, center ice in own zone] WRONG — never turn it over there

options.label: C

images.readTrigger: Net-front body position — own the high-crease real estate before the attacker establishes it.

questions.questionText: You're a defender in your own zone, the opposition is cycling near your net. Where should your eyes be most of the time?

options.label: B
options.text: Always on the puck

options.label: C
options.text: On the goalie

options.label: D
options.text: On the bench for a change

questions.explanation: The puck doesn't score goals — the unattended attacker scores goals.

questions.questionText: Where should your stick be when defending in front of the net?

options.label: A
options.text: On the ice between you and the puck — block the lane

options.label: B
options.text: Up high to block shots

options.label: C

options.label: D

questions.explanation: Stick on ice and on the lane is the foundation of every defensive read.

questions.questionText: Two attackers are net-front and you're defending alone in the slot. Who do you cover first?

options.label: A
options.text: Whichever attacker is closest to the goalie's slot — most dangerous spot

options.label: B
options.text: Whoever has the puck

options.label: C
options.text: Whoever is highest in the zone

options.label: D
options.text: Doesn't matter — pick one

questions.explanation: Closest to the net = most dangerous. Body on them, stick on the lane.

questions.questionText: Why is body position more important than stick position in front of the net?

options.label: A
options.text: It physically prevents the attacker from establishing a screen or tip-in spot

options.label: B
options.text: It looks more aggressive to the ref

options.label: C
options.text: Stick position is never important

options.label: D
options.text: It isn't — stick is always more important

questions.explanation: Body wins position; stick takes the lane. Both matter, body first.

questions.questionText: What's the biggest mistake net-front defenders make?

options.label: A
options.text: Watching the puck instead of their man

options.label: B
options.text: Crowding the goalie

options.label: C
options.text: Being too aggressive

options.label: D
options.text: Standing too tall

questions.explanation: Puck-watchers get scored on. Stay attached to your man.

questions.questionText: A shot from the point bounces off the goalie into the slot. What's your first responsibility?

options.label: A
options.text: Tie up your man's stick so they can't pounce on the rebound

options.label: B
options.text: Skate to the puck yourself

options.label: C
options.text: Yell at the goalie

options.label: D
options.text: Look at the bench

questions.explanation: Tie up sticks first, then look for the puck. Most rebounds are scored by uncovered men.

questions.questionText: When should you leave your check to help a teammate?

options.label: A
options.text: Almost never — your assignment is your responsibility

options.label: B
options.text: Whenever the puck moves

options.label: C
options.text: When you're tired

options.label: D
options.text: Always — help over coverage

questions.explanation: Coverage is built on trust. Leaving your man without communicating creates the open scorer.

questions.questionText: Tap where a defender's stick should be in this DZ-coverage situation.

options.label: B

options.label: C

questions.questionText: Tap the spot where a defender most needs to win body position.

questions.questionText: You tied up the attacker's stick, the goalie has the puck and the whistle is about to blow. What's the next thing the defender should do?

questions.questionText: An attacker is carrying the puck through the neutral zone toward your blue line. As the strong-side defender, what's your priority?

options.label: C

options.label: D

questions.questionText: Defending a 1-on-1 in the slot, where should your stick be?

questions.questionText: How do you tell if a carrier is about to shoot vs pass?

options.label: A

options.label: B

options.label: C

options.text: You can't — it's random
