questions.questionText: On a 1-on-1, you can either go for the body check OR poke the puck. Which is better?

options.label: A
options.text: Stay on body position with stick in the lane — don't lunge for either

options.label: B
options.text: Always go for the big hit

options.label: C
options.text: Always poke

options.label: D
options.text: Skate away

questions.explanation: Lunging for body OR puck loses your gap if you miss. Stay big, body angle, stick on lane. The carrier runs out of options.

questions.questionText: How do you steer the carrier with body position?

options.label: A
options.text: Angle your hips to push them toward the boards — boards become your second defender

options.label: B
options.text: Stand square so they have a clear path

options.label: C
options.text: Stand directly behind them

options.label: D
options.text: Crouch low and look at the puck

questions.explanation: Angle the gap to the outside. The carrier has the boards on one side and you on the other — their lane shrinks.

questions.questionText: On a 1-on-1, do you want to force the carrier outside or invite them to the middle?

options.label: A
options.text: Always force outside — the boards are your friend, the slot is theirs

options.label: B
options.text: Invite them middle — it's easier to defend

options.label: C
options.text: It depends on the score

options.label: D
options.text: Doesn't matter

questions.explanation: Outside = lower-percentage shooting angles + boards squeezing the carrier. Middle = scoring chances.

questions.questionText: You're the first forward back on a 2-on-2 against. The carrier is engaged with your D. What's your responsibility?

options.label: A
options.text: Pick up the highest open trailer — don't double-team the carrier

options.label: B
options.text: Help your D body the carrier

options.label: C
options.text: Head to the bench for a change

options.label: D
options.text: Skate behind your goalie

questions.explanation: Two on the carrier = open trailer. F1 back picks up the late man so the D isn't helpless if the carrier dishes.

questions.questionText: The carrier is winding up like a shot but the trailer is screaming for a drop pass. What's the tell?

options.label: A
options.text: If the carrier's eyes lift to scan, it's a pass coming — stay alert to the trailer's lane

options.label: B
options.text: It's always a shot

options.label: C
options.text: It's always a drop pass

options.label: D
options.text: You can't tell, just block whatever

questions.explanation: Scanning eyes = pass coming. Read the carrier's head and stick angle to anticipate a drop or a one-time pass.

questions.questionText: When should a defender commit to a hit vs. play the position?

options.label: A
options.text: Commit only if you can finish without losing your gap or position

options.label: B
options.text: Always commit — hits change games

options.label: C
options.text: Never commit — it's too risky

options.label: D
options.text: Only on power plays

questions.explanation: A missed hit is a disaster. Body angle and gap is enough — only finish if you're certain.

questions.questionText: What angle should your stick be at when defending a rush?

options.label: A
options.text: Stick parallel to the carrier's stick, blade flat in the passing lane

options.label: B
options.text: Stick high in the air

options.label: C
options.text: Stick straight up and down

options.label: D
options.text: Stick pointing at the goalie

questions.explanation: Parallel to the puck = maximum lane coverage. Blade flat = poke-check ready, lane sealed.

images.readTrigger: Multiple defenders collapsed in the slot — find your assignment, tie up sticks, win body position.

questions.questionText: Multiple defenders are collapsed at net-front. What's the most valuable real estate to own?

options.label: A
options.text: The high crease — between the attacker and the goalie's slot

options.label: B
options.text: Behind the net

options.label: C
options.text: The blue line

options.label: D
options.text: Center ice

questions.explanation: High crease wins tip-ins, screens, and rebounds. Most NHL goals are scored from there — own that body position.

questions.questionText: A point shot is incoming. Your check is parked in the slot. What's your priority?

options.label: A
options.text: Tie up your check's stick — they can't tip or pounce on a rebound if their stick is locked up

options.label: B
options.text: Skate to the goalie to help

options.label: C
options.text: Block the shot yourself

options.label: D
options.text: Skate to the bench

questions.explanation: Tie up sticks first — it neutralizes tips AND rebounds. The goalie can stop the puck if you neutralize the danger man.

questions.questionText: What's the cardinal sin of net-front defense?

options.label: A
options.text: Watching the puck instead of your check

options.label: B
options.text: Crowding the goalie

options.label: C
options.text: Standing too tall

options.label: D
options.text: Communicating with teammates

questions.explanation: Puck watchers lose their man. The unattended attacker scores. Stay attached to your check, scan the puck only briefly.

questions.questionText: Your teammate is fighting a wall battle in the corner and you're net-front. Should you go help?

options.label: A
options.text: No — stay on your check, your teammate will win or lose the puck on their own

options.label: B
options.text: Yes — always help in the corner

options.label: C
options.text: Only if you're tired

options.label: D
options.text: Yes — leave your man to crash the boards

questions.explanation: Coverage is built on trust. Leaving net-front to help in the corner is exactly what creates the open scoring chance.

questions.questionText: Stick on stick or stick in the lane — which is right at net-front?

options.label: A
options.text: Stick on stick when your check is screening or tipping; stick in the lane when the puck is on the perimeter

options.label: B
options.text: Always stick in the lane

options.label: C
options.text: Always stick on stick

options.label: D
options.text: Stick doesn't matter at net-front

questions.explanation: It depends on the threat. A net-front check needs their stick neutralized. A perimeter shooter calls for lane-blocking.

questions.questionText: How do you spot a screen play developing?

options.label: A
options.text: An off-puck attacker is parking themselves directly in the goalie's sight line right before a point shot loads

options.label: B
options.text: Players skating in circles

options.label: C
options.text: The goalie standing up

options.label: D
options.text: Anything happening behind the net

questions.explanation: The screener parks in the goalie's eye line and stays still. As the point loads up, that's your trigger to clear the screener — body them OR move them off the spot.

questions.questionText: How do you box out a screener so the goalie can see?

options.label: A
options.text: Get your body between the goalie's eyes and the screener — push them off the post

options.label: B
options.text: Yell at the goalie to move

options.label: C
options.text: Skate to the corner

options.label: D
options.text: Lean on the goalie

questions.explanation: If your body is between the goalie's view and the screener, the goalie sees the puck. Move the screener if you can; otherwise block their reach with your body.

questions.questionText: A rebound bounces into the slot. What's the FIRST thing the net-front defender does?

options.label: A
options.text: Tie up the closest attacker's stick — don't chase the puck yet

options.label: B
options.text: Dive on the puck

options.label: C
options.text: Skate to the boards

options.label: D
options.text: Yell at the goalie

questions.explanation: Rebounds are scored by uncovered men. Stick-tie first; the goalie or a second defender gets the loose puck.

questions.questionText: What's the 'seam' in net-front defense and how do you close it?

options.label: A
options.text: The diagonal lane between the puck and a backdoor attacker — close it by getting your body in the lane

options.label: B
options.text: The seam in the goalie's pads

options.label: C
options.text: The crack between two faceoff dots

options.label: D
options.text: The line where you switch checks

questions.explanation: The seam is the cross-ice pass lane to a wide-open backdoor man. Body in the lane removes the most dangerous play.

questions.questionText: An attacker switches sides through the slot. Do you switch checks with your D-partner or stay with your original man?

options.label: A
options.text: Switch — but ONLY if your partner is calling 'I got him' so neither of you ends up free

options.label: B
options.text: Always stay — never switch

options.label: C
options.text: Always switch silently

options.label: D
options.text: Both stay on your original — attacker will choose one

questions.explanation: Switches save energy and shorten coverage gaps, but require COMMUNICATION. Silent switches create open men. Always call it.

images.readTrigger: Yellow attackers at the points and high slot — recognize the formation (box vs. diamond) and rotate accordingly.

questions.questionText: Yellow attackers are at both points and you're defending in your own zone. Which formation does your team usually run?

options.label: A
options.text: Box defense — four skaters forming a square, one man each at the points and one at each net-front post

options.label: B
options.text: Single line across the blue line

options.label: C
options.text: All four players in front of the goalie

options.label: D
options.text: Three players behind the net

questions.explanation: Box covers the four high-danger spots: two at the points, two at the net-front posts. Diamond is the alternative — one high, two middle, one low.

questions.questionText: When does a defender pressure the point shooter vs hold position?

options.label: A
options.text: Pressure when the shooter is loading up; hold when they're catching or passing

options.label: B
options.text: Always pressure

options.label: C
options.text: Always hold

options.label: D
options.text: It depends on the score only

questions.explanation: Pressure when a shot is imminent (windup, head down). Hold when the puck is moving through them — jumping out blows your shape.

questions.questionText: What does a proper shot-blocking stance look like for a forward?

options.label: A
options.text: Side-on, knees bent, stick on the ice in front — sweep into the lane as the shot releases

options.label: B
options.text: Squared up to the shooter, standing tall

options.label: C
options.text: Lying flat on your back

options.label: D
options.text: Crouched behind the goalie

questions.explanation: Side-on protects skates and ankles, sweeping stick covers the lane. Standing square is a one-way ticket to a broken foot.

questions.questionText: When should a forward in box defense rotate up to pressure the point?

options.label: A
options.text: When their D-pair calls them up OR when the puck is held at the point with no immediate threat

options.label: B
options.text: Whenever they're tired

options.label: C
options.text: Always — pressure is everything

options.label: D
options.text: Never — stay low forever

questions.explanation: Box rotations are call-and-response with the D-pair. Move only on a call — silent rotations leave gaps.

questions.questionText: A point shot is loading. Do you block it or screen for the goalie's eyes?

options.label: A
options.text: Block it if you're in the lane already; otherwise let the goalie see the puck cleanly

options.label: B
options.text: Always block

options.label: C
options.text: Always screen

options.label: D
options.text: Yell at the goalie

questions.explanation: Half-blocks are the worst outcome — deflects the shot AND screens the goalie. Either commit or get out of the way.

questions.questionText: How can you tell a point shooter is about to fire vs pass down low?

options.label: A
options.text: Stick winds up high and head drops to the puck — shot loading. Eyes scan low — pass coming.

options.label: B
options.text: By the size of their gloves

options.label: C
options.text: By the goalie's face

options.label: D
options.text: You can't tell

questions.explanation: Same tells as carrying. Head down + stick load = shot. Head up scanning = looking for a pass option.

questions.questionText: If your strong-side D pinches up to challenge the point, what does the weak-side defender do?

options.label: A
options.text: Slide across to cover the slot — you're now the last man back

options.label: B
options.text: Pinch with them

options.label: C
options.text: Skate to the bench

options.label: D
options.text: Stay home in the corner

questions.explanation: When your partner pinches, the seam opens on their side. You rotate to the middle to cover the slot.

questions.questionText: A bouncing puck arrives at the point. The shooter has to settle it. What's your move?

options.label: A
options.text: Pressure immediately — a bouncing puck is the easiest to dispossess and the hardest to shoot

options.label: B
options.text: Hold position and hope they fan

options.label: C
options.text: Skate behind the net

options.label: D
options.text: Drop to one knee

questions.explanation: Bouncing pucks at the point are turnover gold. Pressure forces a poor handle, a missed shot, or a clear.

questions.questionText: How do you box out a forward fighting for a rebound near the post?

options.label: A
options.text: Get inside position with your body, stick across their stick, drive them out toward the goal line

options.label: B
options.text: Stand directly in front of them

options.label: C
options.text: Lean on the goalie

options.label: D
options.text: Hold their jersey

questions.explanation: Inside position + stick lift moves the attacker out of the danger zone. Body angle drives them away from the slot.

questions.questionText: What's an 'attacker-D switch' and when does it happen on the PP?

options.label: A
options.text: An attacker drops down and a D rotates up to the point — confuses coverage by changing positions

options.label: B
options.text: Two attackers swap jerseys

options.label: C
options.text: The D becomes the goalie

options.label: D
options.text: It's only a hockey card term

questions.explanation: Top PP units rotate constantly. Watch for a forward dropping into the bumper while a D rotates up — your assignment changes if you don't switch.

images.readTrigger: Two defenders gapped at the top of their circles — the slot opens when you pull one wide.

questions.questionText: You're the middle blue forward carrying the puck across the offensive blue line on a 3-on-2. Your two wingers are wide. The two red defenders are gapped at the top of their circles. What's the best read?

options.label: A
options.text: Drive wide and force a defender to commit

options.label: B
options.text: Dump it in deep and forecheck

options.label: C
options.text: Shoot from the high blue line

options.label: D
options.text: Drop it back to a non-existent trailer

questions.explanation: Forcing a defender to follow you opens the slot for a pass or your own drive.

questions.questionText: Same 3-on-2 entry. Defenders are shrinking the gap fast and you're losing space. When does dumping the puck in actually make sense?

options.label: A
options.text: When the defenders close the gap and have you fully contained

options.label: B
options.text: Every entry — dump-and-chase is the default

options.label: C
options.text: When you have a clear shooting lane

options.label: D
options.text: When a teammate is alone in front of the net

questions.explanation: Dump it only when carrying it costs you possession. Otherwise carry or pass.

questions.questionText: Your right winger is wide open at the boards. The far defender hasn't committed. What's the safer first option for the puck carrier?

options.label: A
options.text: Pass to the wide winger to pull a defender

options.label: B
options.text: Try to beat both defenders 1-on-2 down the middle

options.label: C
options.text: Fire a shot from the top of the circle

options.label: D
options.text: Skate behind the net to slow the play down

questions.explanation: When a defender hasn't committed, move the puck to the open side and make them.

questions.questionText: What's the worst thing the puck carrier can do on this 3-on-2?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop their feet at the blue line

options.label: B
options.text: Drive wide

options.label: C
options.text: Keep their head up and read the defenders

options.label: D
options.text: Communicate with their wingers

questions.explanation: Speed beats defenders. Stopping your feet kills the rush every time.

questions.questionText: Why does drive-wide work so well against gapped defenders?

options.label: A
options.text: It forces a defender to follow you, leaving the slot wide open

options.label: B
options.text: It's just the fastest way to the net

options.label: C
options.text: It eliminates the need to pass

options.label: D
options.text: It puts the puck deeper in the zone for free

questions.explanation: The trade in your favor: you give up the puck-carrier lane to open the slot for a teammate.

questions.questionText: The left red defender steps up aggressively to challenge you. Where's the best pass?

options.label: A
options.text: The right winger on the far side — defender just vacated that lane

options.label: B
options.text: The left winger on the near side

options.label: C
options.text: Back to the goalie

options.label: D
options.text: A drop pass behind you

questions.explanation: When a defender commits early, the cross-ice pass to the weak side is the highest-percentage play.

questions.questionText: Tap the open ice you should drive into.

options.label: A
options.text: [Spot 1, slot middle] CORRECT — slot is open with both defenders gapped wide

options.label: B
options.text: [Spot 2, far wide] WRONG — too far wide, into the boards

options.label: C
options.text: [Spot 3, near goalie] WRONG — goalie's space

options.label: D
options.text: [Spot 4, off-side wide] WRONG — passenger lane

questions.explanation: On a 3-on-2 with gapped defenders, the slot is the prize.

questions.questionText: Tap the defender most likely to step up on you first.

options.label: A
options.text: [Spot 1, strong-side defender] CORRECT — closer pressure point

options.label: B
options.text: [Spot 2, off-side defender] WRONG — holds to cover cross-ice

options.label: C
options.text: [Spot 3, goalie] WRONG — goalie doesn't step up

questions.explanation: The defender on your side will challenge first; the far one stays home to cover the pass.

questions.questionText: Tap your best passing option if you commit to driving wide left.

options.label: A
options.text: [Spot 1, right winger off-side] CORRECT — cross-ice into open ice

options.label: B
options.text: [Spot 2, behind you near-side] WRONG — same side you're attacking

options.label: C
options.text: [Spot 3, goalie] WRONG — not a teammate

questions.explanation: Driving one side opens the cross-ice lane — that's where the goalie can't slide in time.

questions.questionText: Why does the goalie's depth matter on the entry?

options.label: A
options.text: A deep goalie tells you to drive wide; a high goalie tells you to shoot quick

options.label: B
options.text: It doesn't — the goalie is irrelevant on entries

options.label: C
options.text: You should always shoot at the goalie's chest

options.label: D
options.text: You should always pass — never shoot

questions.explanation: Read the goalie like you read the defenders — depth tells you whether to drive or shoot.

images.readTrigger: Player's eyes are level with the rink, looking up the ice at teammates and defenders — not down at the puck on the stick blade.
images.distractors: The puck is sitting flat under the stick blade in clear view in the lower foreground; an obvious temptation to look down. A defender is closing from the right side. Two teammates are visible up ice through the neutral zone.

questions.questionText: You're carrying the puck through the middle of the ice. Where should your eyes be?

options.label: A
options.text: Up, looking at the ice ahead and your teammates

options.label: B
options.text: Down, watching the puck on your stick blade

options.label: C
options.text: On the scoreboard checking the time

options.label: D
options.text: Closed so you can focus on stickhandling

questions.explanation: Eyes up is the foundation of every other hockey skill. When your eyes are up you can see teammates, defenders, and open ice. Looking at the puck means you'll skate straight into trouble.

questions.questionText: Why is it important to look up while you're carrying the puck through the neutral zone?

options.label: A
options.text: So coaches can see your face on the bench

options.label: B
options.text: So you don't trip over the blue line

options.label: C
options.text: So you can see who's open and where pressure is coming from

options.label: D
options.text: So the goalie can see your eyes

questions.explanation: Eyes-up = information. Looking up tells you which teammate is open, where the defender is closing from, and whether you have time to skate or need to make a play. Eyes down = none of that.

questions.questionText: You see a defender closing from the right and two teammates ahead. What does "eyes up" let you do here that "eyes down" wouldn't?

options.label: A
options.text: Skate faster through the neutral zone

options.label: B
options.text: Choose the right pass before the defender takes it away

options.label: C
options.text: Make a tighter turn

options.label: D
options.text: Stickhandle through traffic

questions.explanation: Eyes-up turns information into a decision. You can see the defender's angle and your two options at once — pick the better pass before he closes the lane. Eyes-down players make the play after the defender forces it.

images.readTrigger: Before the pass arrives, the player's head is turned to scan the ice — they already know where the puck and pressure are coming from.
images.distractors: Puck is on a teammate's stick about 25 feet away on the wall, getting ready to pass. A defender is closing on the receiver's blind side. The opponent's net is visible in the upper third — a shooting option exists.

questions.questionText: When should you look around to see what's going on around you?

options.label: A
options.text: Before the pass arrives on your stick

options.label: B
options.text: Only after you've received the puck

options.label: C
options.text: Never — focus on the puck

options.label: D
options.text: Only when the coach yells

questions.explanation: Scanning before the puck arrives is what lets you make a one-touch play. If you only look up after you receive, the defender is already on you.

questions.questionText: Your teammate on the wall is winding up to pass to you. What's the best thing to do RIGHT NOW, before the puck arrives?

options.label: A
options.text: Stare at his stick blade so you don't miss the pass

options.label: B
options.text: Yell for the puck louder

options.label: C
options.text: Check over your shoulder for defenders and open teammates

options.label: D
options.text: Skate harder toward the net

questions.explanation: The window before the pass arrives is when you can scan without losing the puck. A shoulder check tells you who's behind you, who's open, and whether you'll have time to shoot or need to move it again.

questions.questionText: You scanned and saw a defender closing on your blind side. The pass is coming. What's your best play?

options.label: A
options.text: Catch the pass and try to spin off the defender

options.label: B
options.text: Catch and move the puck quickly to a teammate or to space — don't hold it

options.label: C
options.text: Let the pass go and skate away

options.label: D
options.text: Catch the pass and stickhandle

questions.explanation: Knowing pressure is coming changes your play. Move the puck on the first touch instead of trying to beat the defender. The shoulder check is what made this read possible.

images.readTrigger: The puck is meeting the stick blade; the receiver's hands are giving — pulling slightly back with the puck — instead of stabbing at it.
images.distractors: A teammate's stick blade is releasing the puck on the right side of frame. The center red line is visible behind. The receiver's stick blade is flat and angled slightly closed to cushion. A defender is two strides behind.

questions.questionText: The puck is about to hit your stick. What should your hands do?

options.label: A
options.text: Push the stick forward to slap the puck

options.label: B
options.text: Pull the stick back a little — like you're catching an egg

options.label: C
options.text: Squeeze the stick as tight as you can

options.label: D
options.text: Let go of the stick

questions.explanation: Soft hands cushion the pass. Pulling the stick back at the moment of contact takes energy out of the puck so it stays on your blade instead of bouncing off.

questions.questionText: What does giving with your hands when you receive a pass actually do?

options.label: A
options.text: Makes the pass faster

options.label: B
options.text: Surprises the goalie

options.label: C
options.text: Takes the bounce out of the puck so it stays on your stick

options.label: D
options.text: Keeps your gloves clean

questions.explanation: A stiff blade is like a wall — the puck bounces off. A soft, giving blade is like a glove — the puck sticks. Same idea as a fielder catching a baseball.

questions.questionText: What's the best stick blade angle for receiving a pass cleanly?

options.label: A
options.text: Flat on the ice and slightly closed (cupped) toward you

options.label: B
options.text: Lifted off the ice with the blade open

options.label: C
options.text: Vertical pointed at the passer

options.label: D
options.text: Held above your shoulder

questions.explanation: Cupped + on the ice = the puck stays under the blade. Open blade lets it bounce up and over. Vertical or lifted = the puck slides under the blade and is gone.

images.readTrigger: Player's body is between the defender and the puck — back to the defender, puck on the far side of the body along the boards.
images.distractors: Boards are at the edge of frame on the left. A defender's red gloves are visible reaching around. Two teammates are open in the slot waiting for support. The puck is tucked along the boards on the far-side of the body.

questions.questionText: A defender is pressuring you on the wall in the offensive zone. Where should your body be?

options.label: A
options.text: Squared up to the defender so you can fight for the puck

options.label: B
options.text: On the ice for the referee to see

options.label: C
options.text: Between the defender and the puck — body shielding the puck

options.label: D
options.text: Skating away from the defender as fast as you can

questions.explanation: Body between the defender and the puck = puck protection. Squaring up means the defender can reach the puck. Shielding buys you time for support.

questions.questionText: You've shielded the puck from a defender on the wall. What's your job NEXT?

options.label: A
options.text: Try to score from the wall

options.label: B
options.text: Look up and find a teammate cutting to the slot or open space

options.label: C
options.text: Hold the puck on the wall as long as possible

options.label: D
options.text: Pass the puck back to your goalie

questions.explanation: Puck protection isn't the goal — it's the bridge. Once the puck's safe, your eyes go up and find the teammate who's now open because the defender is on you.

questions.questionText: The defender is reaching around your body trying to poke the puck. Where exactly should the puck be?

options.label: A
options.text: On the FAR side of your body from the defender — your torso between him and the puck

options.label: B
options.text: On the SAME side as the defender's stick

options.label: C
options.text: Right at your skates

options.label: D
options.text: Behind your back

questions.explanation: Puck protection is geometric. Put your body in the line between the defender's stick and the puck. If the puck is on the same side as the defender, no body in the way = easy poke.

images.readTrigger: Player has turned their back to the on-coming defender, shielding the puck with the body while looking up ice for support.
images.distractors: Center red line is visible across mid-frame. An opposing forechecker is closing fast from the front. A teammate in navy is curling for support up ice. The puck is tucked behind the protected hip.

questions.questionText: A forechecker is closing on you fast in the neutral zone. What's your best move?

options.label: A
options.text: Skate straight at him to dump and chase

options.label: B
options.text: Stop dead and wait

options.label: C
options.text: Turn your back to him and shield the puck while you find support

options.label: D
options.text: Try to deke around him at full speed

questions.explanation: Turning away buys you two seconds for your support to arrive. Skating into him means a check; deking at speed often means a turnover. Turn, protect, wait for help.

questions.questionText: Why is turning away from a forechecker often better than skating forward through him?

options.label: A
options.text: It's the rule in youth hockey

options.label: B
options.text: Turning makes him commit to one side, so you can release the puck to support OR move the other way once he passes

options.label: C
options.text: It looks fancier

options.label: D
options.text: It makes you faster

questions.explanation: Turning is a delay tactic that creates options. The forechecker has to commit to one side; once he does, you have the other side AND the support pass available.

questions.questionText: You've turned your back and shielded the puck from the forechecker. What should you be looking for next?

options.label: A
options.text: A teammate curling for support — pass to him while the forechecker is committed

options.label: B
options.text: A way to skate past the forechecker

options.label: C
options.text: The bench so you can get a line change

options.label: D
options.text: Your goalie

questions.explanation: Puck protection without support is just a stall. The whole point of buying time is so a teammate can curl into a passing lane. Find him, hit him with the pass, keep the play moving.

images.readTrigger: Both teams settling into a regroup pattern across the blue / red lines — read the forecheck and find the open lane.

questions.questionText: You're regrouping in the neutral zone with a soft forecheck pressing your D-pair. What's the safest first option?

options.label: A
options.text: D-to-D pass to switch sides and reset the regroup

options.label: B
options.text: Hard rim around the boards

options.label: C
options.text: Stretch pass to a high forward

options.label: D
options.text: Skate hard up the middle through traffic

questions.explanation: When the forecheck is soft and you have time, D-to-D resets the read and forces the forecheckers to commit again.

questions.questionText: Two opposing forwards are pressing your D and one is hanging high. Which forecheck shape is that?

options.label: A
options.text: 1-2-2 — one deep, two in mid-zone, two back

options.label: B
options.text: 2-1-2 — two deep forecheckers

options.label: C
options.text: Neutral-zone trap

options.label: D
options.text: No forecheck at all

questions.explanation: 1-2-2 keeps one forechecker deep and the next layer through the neutral zone. The cure is a stretch pass or D-to-D to bypass the second layer.

questions.questionText: Your D has the puck in the regroup. Where should the wings be?

options.label: A
options.text: Wide along the boards on the same side, ready to receive a wall pass

options.label: B
options.text: Behind your own net helping the goalie

options.label: C
options.text: Crashing into the slot in your D-zone

options.label: D
options.text: Sitting in the offensive blue line waiting

questions.explanation: Wings on the wall give the D-pair a short, low-risk outlet. Crashing too deep crowds the puck.

questions.questionText: When does D-to-D across the back become the right play during a regroup?

options.label: A
options.text: When the forecheck commits to one side and the off-side D has open ice

options.label: B
options.text: Every time you touch the puck

options.label: C
options.text: When you want to slow the play down to bait icing

options.label: D
options.text: Never — D-to-D is always too risky

questions.explanation: D-to-D is a redirection move — you switch sides to attack the soft side the forecheck just abandoned.

questions.questionText: What does triangle support look like during a regroup?

options.label: A
options.text: Three options for the puck carrier: same-side winger, center, and the off-side D

options.label: B
options.text: All five players in a straight line

options.label: C
options.text: Goalie + 4 skaters in a circle

options.label: D
options.text: Only the puck carrier and the goalie

questions.explanation: Triangle = three outlets at all times. Carrier in the middle, two passing options forming a triangle around them.

questions.questionText: When does the stretch pass to a high forward become the right call?

options.label: A
options.text: When all opposing forecheckers are below the puck and your high forward is alone

options.label: B
options.text: Always — stretch pass is the default

options.label: C
options.text: When the goalie has the puck

options.label: D
options.text: When the score is tied

questions.explanation: Stretch beats every forechecker in one pass IF you've identified the opening. With forecheckers above the puck it's a turnover.

questions.questionText: What's the right tempo during a regroup?

options.label: A
options.text: Slow down enough to read the forecheck, then accelerate when you've made the decision

options.label: B
options.text: Always full speed — never slow down

options.label: C
options.text: Stop completely until everyone is ready

options.label: D
options.text: Skate backwards

questions.explanation: Pace = control. Read first, then attack with speed when the lane opens.

questions.questionText: The closest opposing player is gapped tight on your D's near hip. What's the read?

options.label: A
options.text: Move the puck immediately — the carrier is about to be pressured into a turnover

options.label: B
options.text: Hold the puck and wait for support

options.label: C
options.text: Skate straight at the defender

options.label: D
options.text: Pass to the goalie

questions.explanation: When pressure closes the gap, you've already lost the time to read — move the puck to the open option fast.

questions.questionText: Who is the F3 (third forward) during a regroup, and where should they be?

options.label: A
options.text: The high forward swinging through the middle as the safety valve and stretch option

options.label: B
options.text: The forward camped in front of the opposing goalie

options.label: C
options.text: There is no F3 — only F1 and F2 exist

options.label: D
options.text: The defenseman on the off-side

questions.explanation: F3 = high middle support. They're the outlet if everything below breaks down AND the trigger for a stretch.

questions.questionText: What is an offside trap during a regroup, and how do you beat it?

options.label: A
options.text: The opposition holds their blue line to force you offside — beat it with delayed entries and timing crossovers

options.label: B
options.text: It's when the ref is offside

options.label: C
options.text: It's when you trap your own player by accident

options.label: D
options.text: It only happens during PK

questions.explanation: Trap teams stand up at their own blue line to force a turnover at the line. Counter: chip and chase, or delay the rush so wingers stay onside.

images.readTrigger: Knees are bent, hips are low, weight is centered over the balls of the feet — ready to move in any direction.
images.distractors: The faceoff dot is visible directly under the stick blade. An opposing player is across the dot in the same low stance. A linesman's skates are in the periphery.

questions.questionText: What does an athletic hockey stance look like?

options.label: A
options.text: Standing tall with straight legs and stick raised

options.label: B
options.text: Sitting flat on the ice waiting for the puck

options.label: C
options.text: Knees bent, hips low, weight on the balls of your feet, stick on the ice

options.label: D
options.text: Leaning all your weight on one skate

questions.explanation: Athletic stance is the foundation of every move you make on the ice — stops, starts, crossovers, contact. Knees bent, weight balanced, stick down.

questions.questionText: Why do good hockey players bend their knees?

options.label: A
options.text: It looks cool

options.label: B
options.text: It lets them move quicker, stop faster, and stay strong on the puck

options.label: C
options.text: It makes them shorter so the goalie can't see them

options.label: D
options.text: It stops the ice from being slippery

questions.explanation: Bent knees mean you can push off in any direction in a fraction of a second. A tall, stiff player can only go where they're already pointed.

questions.questionText: On a faceoff, what's the problem with standing tall instead of being in athletic stance?

options.label: A
options.text: You can't see the puck drop

options.label: B
options.text: The ref will throw you out

options.label: C
options.text: Your stick is too long

options.label: D
options.text: You'll lose the puck — you can't pull it back fast enough or hold position against the other player

questions.explanation: Faceoffs are won on the first move and on body leverage. A tall player has nothing to push against, can't move quickly, and gets pushed off the dot.

images.readTrigger: Knees bent, hips back, stick on the ice between the player and the attacker — ready to defend without lunging.
images.distractors: An opposing forward is in the slot setting up for a tip. The puck is on the opposite wall. The goalie is set in the crease just behind. A second attacker is at the net front.

questions.questionText: You're defending the slot in your own end. What should your body and stick be doing?

options.label: A
options.text: Standing straight up so you look bigger

options.label: B
options.text: Knees bent, stick flat on the ice between you and the attacker

options.label: C
options.text: Lunging at the attacker to take the puck

options.label: D
options.text: Skating away from the slot to find help

questions.explanation: Defending in the slot is patient work. Bent knees keep you ready to move. Stick on the ice covers the lane between you and the attacker without committing.

questions.questionText: Why is lunging at the attacker a bad idea when you're defending the slot?

options.label: A
options.text: You might trip

options.label: B
options.text: The ref will call a penalty

options.label: C
options.text: If you miss, you're out of position and the attacker has the lane to the net

options.label: D
options.text: It uses too much energy

questions.explanation: Lunging is a one-shot move — if it doesn't work, you're done. Patient stance with stick on ice forces the attacker to make a play around you, which buys time for help.

questions.questionText: Your stick is on the ice in front of you while you defend the slot. What's the stick actually doing?

options.label: A
options.text: Taking away the pass to the slot attacker — making the carrier choose another option

options.label: B
options.text: Holding you up so you don't fall

options.label: C
options.text: Showing the ref you're playing fair

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing — it's just a habit

questions.explanation: A defensive stick on the ice is your fifth skater. It blocks the cross-ice pass without you having to move. The carrier is forced to take a worse play.

images.readTrigger: Stick blade is flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck carrier and the open teammate — taking away the pass without leaving the feet.
images.distractors: Puck is on the opposing wall on a red-jersey carrier. An open red teammate stands in the slot looking for a pass. The blue line is visible across the upper half of the frame. The goalie is set behind in the crease.

questions.questionText: Where should your stick be when you're defending in your own end?

options.label: A
options.text: Up high so you can swing it

options.label: B
options.text: Flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck and an open opponent

options.label: C
options.text: Tucked under your arm

options.label: D
options.text: Held behind your back

questions.explanation: Stick on ice in the passing lane is the cheapest, highest-value defensive habit. It blocks passes without you having to skate or hit anyone.

questions.questionText: What does keeping your stick flat on the ice take away from the other team?

options.label: A
options.text: Their goalie

options.label: B
options.text: Their faceoff

options.label: C
options.text: Their pass — the stick fills the line between two of their players

options.label: D
options.text: Their offside

questions.explanation: Your stick is a literal shadow on the ice. It physically blocks the passing line between the carrier and an open teammate. The carrier sees the lane closed and has to pick a worse play.

questions.questionText: Which is a better defensive habit?

options.label: A
options.text: Stick flat on the ice in the lane, ready to deflect a pass

options.label: B
options.text: Stick raised in the air, ready to swing

options.label: C
options.text: Stick on your hip while you watch the puck

options.label: D
options.text: Stick blade vertical pointed at the carrier

questions.explanation: A flat blade on the ice covers the lane all by itself. Raised, hipped, or vertical sticks give the carrier a free pass through space.

images.readTrigger: Stick blade is flat on the ice presenting a clear target where the player wants to receive the puck.
images.distractors: A teammate in navy is winding up to pass from the right wall. A defender in red is closing from behind the receiver. The opponent's goalie is in the crease. Faceoff circle markings are on the ice.

questions.questionText: You're open and want a teammate to pass you the puck. Where should your stick be?

options.label: A
options.text: Up high above your head

options.label: B
options.text: Flat on the ice where you want the pass

options.label: C
options.text: Behind your skates

options.label: D
options.text: Tucked into your glove

questions.explanation: Your stick blade on the ice is a target. It tells your teammate exactly where to put the pass — and it's already in the right spot to catch it.

questions.questionText: What helps your teammate make a great pass to you?

options.label: A
options.text: Yelling really loudly

options.label: B
options.text: Skating back and forth so they see you

options.label: C
options.text: Standing perfectly still

options.label: D
options.text: Putting your stick blade flat on the ice exactly where you want the puck

questions.explanation: A passer needs a target. Stick blade flat on the ice = obvious target. Yelling or moving without a stick target makes the passer guess.

questions.questionText: Your stick is flat on the ice as a target — why is that better than holding it up off the ice?

options.label: A
options.text: A flat blade catches a pass cleanly; a lifted blade lets the puck slide under and roll away

options.label: B
options.text: It's against the rules to lift your stick

options.label: C
options.text: It saves energy

options.label: D
options.text: It tricks the goalie

questions.explanation: A blade on the ice is both a target AND a catching surface. A blade lifted even an inch lets the puck pass under it — pass missed, possession lost.
